Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd listen to lectures back-to-back for hours, usually late at night when my roommates were asleep. I learn best by hearing things, so I'd put on lectures while doing other stuff sometimes. I never really had a consistent schedule—I'd just study when I felt like it or when something clicked that I needed to know more about.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything, then suddenly I'd realize I was behind and cram a ton of content in a few days. Deadlines didn't motivate me as much as you'd think—I mean, look at my assignment completion rate. It was more about my mood and energy levels. If I was feeling it, I'd dive deep. If not, the course just sat there.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I wanted to watch a specific video or listen to a lecture I'd been thinking about. Sometimes I'd get curious about a topic we'd briefly covered and want to explore it more. Occasionally peer interactions would pull me back in—like if someone posted something interesting in the discussions. It wasn't really about checking boxes for me.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of logging in most days, mainly because I found a few lecture series that genuinely interested me. I'd watch them during breakfast or while commuting. But that routine fell apart when my extracurriculars got intense and stress started building up. It never became a true habit that stuck.
Q5: The video lectures were everything for me. I'm an auditory learner, so being able to listen and re-listen to explanations was crucial. I'd often play them at 1.5x speed and pause when something important came up. The discussion boards were occasionally useful too—hearing other perspectives helped concepts click sometimes.
Q6: I basically ignored the written assignments and a lot of the reading materials. The readings felt tedious and I couldn't absorb information that way. I know my assignment completion was terrible, but I just couldn't bring myself to do them. They felt disconnected from what I was actually learning through the lectures. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it actually taught me something new or made me think differently. I didn't care about points or grades as much as whether I felt smarter afterward. If I could listen to something and have it spark a new idea or connection, that was valuable. Busywork that just checked a box? I'd skip it without guilt.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff actually felt more appealing sometimes because there was no pressure attached. Required activities felt like obligations, which made me resist them. With optional content, I could explore at my own pace without anxiety about deadlines or grades. The irony is I probably engaged more deeply with optional materials than required assignments.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lectures and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while working out. I had a notebook where I'd jot down concepts that stuck with me from what I heard. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos on related topics. A lot of my actual learning happened completely outside the LMS.
Q10: There were tons of times like that. I'd download a week's worth of lecture audio and study from those files for days without logging in once. Or I'd discuss course concepts with friends over coffee—we'd debate ideas for hours. My exam prep was mostly me listening to downloaded content and talking through concepts out loud to myself. None of that showed up anywhere.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video once, download the audio or take notes, and then never go back to the LMS version. I preferred having materials locally so I could study without internet or distractions. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content five or six more times offline.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My stress levels spiked, I had extracurricular commitments piling up, and I started feeling this moderate anxiety about falling behind. The CBT sessions I was doing helped me cope, but engagement still dropped. When I'm overwhelmed, I tend to retreat rather than push through—it's a pattern I'm aware of but struggle to break.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, the LMS felt like this overwhelming mountain I couldn't climb. I'd log in, see all the tasks I hadn't done, and just close the tab. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant the course often took a backseat. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those modules entirely rather than seeking help.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a lecture playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be stressed about other things or just mentally checked out. I might watch 30 minutes of video and retain nothing because I wasn't really present. The engagement was surface-level at best during those moments.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was the format—written work just doesn't suit how I learn. Part of it was this weird avoidance thing where the more important something was, the more anxious it made me, so I'd push it away. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow never came. My resilience usually kicks in for exams, but assignments? Not so much.
Q16: Deadlines created these panic spikes where I'd suddenly engage intensely for a day or two. But mostly, if a deadline passed, I'd just accept the loss and move on rather than stress about late submissions. I know that's not ideal, but chasing every deadline felt impossible with everything else going on. I'd prioritize exam prep over assignment deadlines every time.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss. When I actually got feedback on the few things I submitted, it sometimes motivated me to engage more with similar content. But often feedback came too late to feel relevant, or I'd already moved on mentally. Positive feedback on discussion posts encouraged me to participate more in those, at least for a while.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could binge content when motivated. Bad because without rigid structure, I'd drift and lose track of where I should be. Having 15 online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and this course's flexibility meant it often got deprioritized.
Q19: The video-heavy content made engagement easier since that's my preferred learning mode. But the assignment structure made it harder—lots of written work that didn't match how I process information. Also, the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to give this one consistent attention. Clearer audio-based alternatives for assignments would have helped me a lot.
Q20: That's basically me. I'd watch tons of videos, participate in discussions, interact with peers—all that shows high activity. But when it came to actually submitting work, I just couldn't follow through. It's not that I wasn't learning; I was deeply engaged with the content. I just struggled to translate that engagement into completed assignments. The learning and the output are separate things.
Q21: That resonates too, in a different way. My exam score was high even though my overall LMS activity was inconsistent. I studied effectively offline, understood the material deeply through listening and discussion, and performed well when it mattered. Low visible activity doesn't mean low learning—it might just mean someone learns differently or studies outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone's actually absorbing content or just letting it play in the background. They miss offline study, conversations with peers, the thinking that happens away from the screen. Logs capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning—wanting to understand it, thinking about it outside of class, connecting it to other things you know. It's not about logging in every day or completing every task. For me, engagement is when I'm genuinely curious and actively processing ideas, even if that doesn't look like traditional participation.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually thought about course concepts during my daily life—on walks, during workouts, in conversations. It wouldn't capture the audio files I listened to repeatedly offline or the peer discussions that happened outside the platform. It also wouldn't show my internal struggle with anxiety and stress that affected my visible participation. The data tells a story, but it's missing most of the chapters.
